coredata.dc:title: Red and green fluorescent carbon dots for highly effective ultraviolet radiation screening and sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lukprang, Tanachporn
Preechaburana, Pakorn
Amloy, Supaluck
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted intense attention because of their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. CDs have a great potential to be used as fluorescent sensors and ultraviolet (UV)-blocking materials. In this research, CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using mangosteen leaves as a precursor. A CD aqueous solution exhibited bright green (G-CDs) and red (R-CDs) fluorescence with naked-eye observation under UV irradiation. The maximum fluorescence emission peak intensities of G-CDs and R-CDs were observed at 312 nm and 671 nm under excitation wavelengths of 275 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The absorbance spectra of CDs are shown in the UV range of 200–400 nm. The transmission spectra indicate that the G-CD solution screens UVB and UVC, while R-CDs are an excellent block for all UVA, UVB, and UVC. The average UV-shielding efficiencies of R-CDs and G-CDs are 97.4 %, and 56.1%, respectively. Furthermore, the R-CDs have a tendency to screen high-energy blue light. FTIR results display functional groups of organic substance on CD surfaces showing that the CDs have good biocompatibility with organics and biological materials. Moreover, nanocomposite films of CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymer (CDs/PVA) were prepared using a drop-casting method. In the polymerized state, R-CDs/PVA and G-CDs/PVA exhibit significant improvements in average UV ray blockage of 100.0%, and 83.8% enhancement, respectively. It is noteworthy that PVA combined with R-CDs achieves complete UV ray blocking. Furthermore, the emissions from both CD solution and CDs/PVA under UV radiation cover the regions of red, green, and blue emission. Hence, this work demonstrates the performance of R-CDs- and G-CDs-based platforms that show a high potential for UV-shielding and sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
UV-Radiation screening
UV detector
Fluorescence
Mangosteen

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fundamental Fund fiscal year 2024, Thammasat University (Contract No. TUFF26/2567) and Thammasat University Research Unit in Innovation of Optical Devices and Nanomaterials for Chemical and Biological Sensing. T. Lukprang would like to thank the Doctoral Scholarship for Research Academic, and Internationalization and Education Research Promotion Scholarship from Thammasat University. The authors would like to thank Department of Physics and Department of Materials Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University for providing access to the optical characterization instruments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis process of CDs.
(a) and (b) are TEM images of R-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. The upper left images are HR-TEM images and the lower left images are distribution histograms.
(a) Normalized Raman intensity spectra and (b) Fourier-transform infrared spectra of R-CDs and G-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and (b) FL emission spectra of CDs. The lower inset of (a) displays a magnified absorption peak at 671 nm. The upper insets of (a) and (b) demonstrate photoemission under visible and UV light, respectively. (c) a CIE chromaticity coordinate diagram of CDs.

Three-dimension fluorescent emission spectra for (a) R-CDs, (b) G-CDs, and (c) RG-CDs with different excitation wavelengths at 270–450 nm. (d)–(f) Schematic diagrams showing the energy level alignment of CDs corresponding to the optimal excitation energy (solid arrows) for maximum emission (dashed arrows).

(a) The transmission spectra of the CDs, the inset shows energy gap fittings with a Tauc plot. (b) Fluorescence emission lifetime spectra of R-CDs and RG-CDs for excitation wavelength at 635 nm, and for G-CDs with the excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

The absorption spectra of (a) R-CDs, (b) G-CDs, and (c) RG-CDs. The transmission spectra of (d) R-CDs, (e) G-CDs, and (f) RG-CDs, respectively. Both absorption and transmission spectra from (a) to (f) are measured by varying the exposure time from 0 to 320 min under irradiation with UVA (405 nm). The insets of (a)–(c) show the absorbance intensities and (d)–(f) the blocking values with exposure time dependence.

Fourier-transform infrared spectra of (a) R-CDs, R-CDs/PVA, and PVA, and that of (b) G-CDs, G-CDs/PVA, and PVA before and after 405 nm UV exposure. (c) The transmission spectra of the R-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs/PVA, G-CDs/PVA, and PVA before and after 405 nm UV exposure. (d) Energy gap fittings with a Tauc plot.

The FL spectra of (a) R-CDs, (b) G-CDs, and (c) RG-CDs after irradiation with UVA (405 nm) with an exposure time from 0 to 320 min. The upper insets of (a)–(c) show the FL intensity variations over times. The lower inset of (a) shows the zoomed-in spectra from 460 to 540 nm for R-CDs.

The pictures of (a) R-CD/PVA and G-CD/PVA films under UVA (405 nm) irradiation with exposure times from 0 to 1010 min. (b) and (c) Color intensity in various UV exposure times obtained from the circle image areas in (a) using RGB color space. (d) CIE chromaticity coordinate diagram of FL emission for R-CD/PVA and G-CD/PVA films under UVA (405 nm) with exposure times from 0 to 1010 min. FL intensities of (e) R-CD/PVA and (f) G-CD/PVA films before and after UV exposure.
UV blocking values of pure CDs, PVA, and CD/PVA nanocomposite films in the UVA, UVB, and UVC regions.

row.entry: BD a between R-CDs/PVA and R-CDs

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 3 blocking values of UVA, UVB, and UVC.

head.ce:title: Red and green fluorescent carbon dots for highly effective ultraviolet radiation screening and sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Thammasat University Research Unit in Innovation of Optical Devices and Nanomaterials for chemical and biological sensing, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Thammasat University Research Unit in Innovation of Optical Devices and Nanomaterials for chemical and biological sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Thammasat University Research Unit in Innovation of Optical Devices and Nanomaterials for chemical and biological sensing, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand.
Corresponding author. Department of Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted intense attention because of their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. CDs have a great potential to be used as fluorescent sensors and ultraviolet (UV)-blocking materials. In this research, CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using mangosteen leaves as a precursor. A CD aqueous solution exhibited bright green (G-CDs) and red (R-CDs) fluorescence with naked-eye observation under UV irradiation. The maximum fluorescence emission peak intensities of G-CDs and R-CDs were observed at 312 nm and 671 nm under excitation wavelengths of 275 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The absorbance spectra of CDs are shown in the UV range of 200–400 nm. The transmission spectra indicate that the G-CD solution screens UVB and UVC, while R-CDs are an excellent block for all UVA, UVB, and UVC. The average UV-shielding efficiencies of R-CDs and G-CDs are 97.4 %, and 56.1%, respectively. Furthermore, the R-CDs have a tendency to screen high-energy blue light. FTIR results display functional groups of organic substance on CD surfaces showing that the CDs have good biocompatibility with organics and biological materials. Moreover, nanocomposite films of CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymer (CDs/PVA) were prepared using a drop-casting method. In the polymerized state, R-CDs/PVA and G-CDs/PVA exhibit significant improvements in average UV ray blockage of 100.0%, and 83.8% enhancement, respectively. It is noteworthy that PVA combined with R-CDs achieves complete UV ray blocking. Furthermore, the emissions from both CD solution and CDs/PVA under UV radiation cover the regions of red, green, and blue emission. Hence, this work demonstrates the performance of R-CDs- and G-CDs-based platforms that show a high potential for UV-shielding and sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure can be hazardous to human skin, increasing the risk of skin cancer and causing serious health problems. In order to protect human skin against UV radiation, inorganic UV absorbent nanomaterials, such as titanium dioxide (TiO2) and zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles, are added to sunscreen cosmetics (Ghamarpoor et al., 2023). However, harmful chemicals from their synthesis can lead to skin damage. Therefore, several studies have shown green syntheses for both TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles. ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized using Solanum Lycopersicum fruit juice (Elbrolesy et al., 2023). TiO2 nanoparticles were prepared from potato peels (Girigoswami et al., 2024) and a green tea extraction (Ou et al., 2023). However, UV protection products should be concerned with having a nontoxic chemical composition and being environmentally friendly; therefore, the utilization of inorganic UV absorbents remains limited.

There is an urgent and significant need to develop a biodegradable and non-toxic UV absorbent that exhibits outstanding UV absorption and transparency in visible light. Thus, UV absorbents derived from natural sources such as carbon dots (CDs) could be considered due to their high absorbance of UV radiation, multi-color fluorescence, low toxicity, low environmental risk, lower cost, simplicity of production, and good solubility in aqueous and organic solvents (Liu et al., 2020). There are several methods to improve CDs for UV screening applications. Pectin CD films (Ezati and Rhim, 2022), the incorporation of nitrogen-doped CDs into a cellulose matrix (Barman et al., 2021), and transparent polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-based composites embedding CDs (Alas et al., 2022; Ardini et al., 2023; Barman et al., 2021; Ezati et al., 2022; Hess et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019; Park et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2021a; Zhao et al., 2022) all exhibit prominent UV blocking properties. Additionally, doping CDs, such as with nitrogen (Barman et al., 2020), CaCO3 (Qiu et al., 2022), and co-doped boron and nitrogen (Zuo et al., 2019), can significantly improve the UV shielding of CDs in combination with PVA films. However, these CDs collaborate with polymers to enhance UV-blocking properties.

Herein, we report a one-pot synthesis for producing both red and green emission CD solutions using the same carbon source, mangosteen leaves, without any doping. The novelty of this work lies in the utilization of different solvents, deionized water, and ethanol capable of extracting different surface functional groups of CDs. As a result, the red and green emission CD solutions exhibit effective UV sensing and screening against UV exposure without the need for any doping. Moreover, their properties make them highly promising for UV-shielding and sensing applications.

The mangosteen was purchased from a local market (Krabi, Thailand). The solvents used were deionized water (17.0 MΩ-cm) and absolute ethanol (AR Grade), which were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Merck, Germany). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) for the nanocomposite films was bought from the DAEJUNG Co., Ltd. (Korea).

The size and morphology of CDs were analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) on a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The Raman spectra were recorded at a 530 nm excitation wavelength using a DXR3 Raman microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA., USA). The chemical functionalities were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd., Manchester, UK) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy on an IRSpirit Shimadzu (Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence recombination lifetime analysis was performed on a FluoroMax® Plus (Horiba, Japan), while the UV–visible absorption and fluorescence (FL) emission spectra were measured on a Duetta spectrofluorometer (Horiba).

The R-CDs and G-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using mangosteen leaves as a carbon source. First, the mangosteen leaves were washed with deionized (DI) water and dried at room temperature. Then, the leaves were chopped and blended in a mixer. Next, 10 g of blended leaves were mixed with 100 mL of absolute ethanol for R-CDs and DI water for G-CDs and kept stirring for 3 h (see Fig. 1 ). After that, the mixture was filtered through 11 μm filter paper to remove the residues. The extraction solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the solution was cooled down to room temperature. Then, the brown and yellowish solutions of R-CDs and G-CDs were purified using microfilter paper with sizes of 0.45 μm and 0.22 μm, respectively, to remove any large particles. Finally, the R-CDs and G-CDs solutions were stored in the dark at 4o C for further use.

Briefly, PVA powder (1 g) was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water and kept stirring at 70 °C for 15 min to dissolve the PVA. A transparent PVA solution was cooled to 30 °C, and 15 mL of CD aqueous solution was added under continuous stirring for 2 h to obtain a slightly yellow viscous solution. After that, the CD/PVA film was prepared by drop-casting method into a Petri dish and dried at room temperature.

The identification of sizes and morphologies of the two typical CDs was examined using HR-TEM. The results demonstrated that the CDs are near-spherical in shape and uniformly distributed, with average sizes of approximately 6.82 ± 1.03 nm and 3.51 ± 0.29 nm for R-CDs and G-CDs, respectively, along with a d-spacing of 0.26 nm and 0.19 nm for each type, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b). Their size distribution histograms, which were obtained using the Image J software for statistical analysis of about 100 particles, have been displayed in the insets of Fig. 2.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the Raman spectra of R-CDs and G-CDs. The Raman peak at 1560 cm−1 represents the G-peak of an sp2-hybridized C atom stretching vibration. The Raman peak at 1342 cm−1 represents the D-peak of an sp3-hybridized C atom defect, which results from the structural defects produced by the bonding of hydroxyl and epoxide groups on the surface (Yi et al., 2022). The peak intensity ratios of the D-band to that of the G-band (ID/IG) for R-CDs and G-CDs are 0.84 and 0.79, respectively. These resulting ratios reflect that the degree of conjugation of sp2-hybridization increases more than with sp3 hybridization for both R-CDs and G-CDs (Wahab et al., 2015). It can also be suggested that the R-CDs have more surface defect states than G-CDs. This is consistent with the FTIR and intrinsic fluorescent emission results. Moreover, the Raman intensity of R-CDs is higher than that of G-CDs due to differences in their concentrations, energy band gaps, and functional groups, which give rise to distinct Raman scattering processes (Wu et al., 2018).

The existence of functional groups on the surface of R-CDs and G-CDs was studied using FTIR spectroscopy, and the resulting spectra are shown in Fig. 3(b). The broad band in the region of 3200–3500 cm−1 is caused by the O–H stretching of hydroxyl groups. The peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2863 cm−1 are attributed to C–H bonds. Furthermore, the stretching vibration of CO bonds at around 1703 cm−1 is identified as the carbonyl group. The peak at 1604–1620 cm−1 attributed to CC bounds is possibly ascribed to aromatic structures of carbon cores. However, these FTIR results suggest that the CDs are rich in oxygen containing groups originated from C–O and C–O–C bonds on the surface due to stretching vibration peaks at 1265–1276 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1. The presence of hydroxyl groups with a peak at 1369–1448 cm−1 indicates C–OH bonds. Additionally, the appearance of a strong C–OH peak at 1448 cm−1 corresponds to the hydrogen bonding of the R-CDs, which is beneficial for enhancing their fluorescence properties. Hence, the hydrogen bonds such as C–H, and C–OH were observed only for R-CDs. The presence of hydrogen bonding creates emission energy traps that increase radiative recombination induced by the electron-hole pairs in R-CDs, and it makes the numbers of photons emitted increase, resulting in lower energy emission (red emission) (Xu et al., 2021b)

To expand the exploration of the optical properties of CDs, the mixing ratio of R-CDs and G-CDs at 1:1 (labeled RG-CDs) was also investigated. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra (Fig. 4 (a)) demonstrate two major absorption bands. The range of 250–350 nm is assigned to the electronic π→π* transition of aromatic sp2 carbon domains (CC, carbon core). A weak broad absorption exhibited from 400 to 470 nm could be attributed to the electronic n→π* transition of the unsaturated groups with oxygen (CO, C–O). Interestingly, a small absorption peak at 671 nm was found in both R-CDs and RG-CDs (see the lower inset in Fig. 4(a)), which could be assigned to the transition of hydrogen bonding, as confirmed by FTIR results. Thus, it can be implied that this emission originates from the trapping of the excited state energy of the C–H/C–OH surface state. (Alas et al., 2022; Barati et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2017; Paul et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021b). In addition, the CD aqueous solutions (color under daylight shown in the inset in Fig. 4(a)) strongly emit red, brown, and green light under the irradiation of a UV lamp at 405 nm for R-CDs, RG-CDs, and G-CDs, respectively, (inset in Fig. 4(b)). The emitted lights correspond to the prominent fluorescence (FL) peaks (Fig. 4(b)) of G-CDs and R-CDs that are 488 nm and 671 nm, respectively. RG-CDs demonstrate the emission peak in both red (671 nm) and green (469 nm) emission ranges. Additionally, the CIE (Commission Internationale de L’Éclairage) chromaticity values were calculated from the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The CIE coordinates are located at (0.21, 0.33), (0.32, 0.23), and (0.48, 0.29) for G-CDs, RG-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. Interestingly, the CIE coordinates of the RG-CDs mixture solution are located between those of the two pure red and green CDs. Thus, it can be implied that another straightforward method for achieving multi-color light sensing in optoelectronic devices is by controlling the phosphor composition.

To study the emission mechanism of CDs, FL emission with varying excitation wavelengths from 270 to 450 nm was performed. Fig. 5 (a), (b), and (c) show the redshift in the emission with an increase in the excitation wavelength. The R-CDs emission demonstrates an optimal maximum emission at 671 nm for an excitation wavelength of 410 nm, while emission in G-CDs at 312 nm reaches a maximum for an excitation wavelength of 275 nm. Furthermore, RG-CDs show three optimal maximum emissions at 671, 438, and 358 nm for excitation wavelengths of 410, 365, and 315 nm, respectively (emission energy states shown in Fig. 5(d)–(f)). These CDs consist of different surface functional groups, especially RG-CDs, which can have carbon-hydrogen and oxygen-hydrogen bonding, causing a different rearrangement of the emission center. Thus, their emission wavelengths are dependent on their surface energy states originating red, green, and brown radiation under UV excitation, all of which can be observed by the naked eye (Zhu et al., 2019).

The transmission spectra of the CDs were measured, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The transmittances of the R-CDs and G-CDs decrease in the UV radiation region. To evaluate the UV-shielding properties, transmittance spectra were employed to determine the blocking activity of the synthesized CDs, pure PVA, and CDs/PVA nanocomposite films. The UV blocking values were calculated using equation (1) (Barman et al., 2021):
(1)
Blocking
%
= 100 −
∫ x y
T
λ
d λ
∫ x y
d λ
where T(λ) represents the average transmittance of each wavelength, λ is the wavelength, and dλ denotes the bandwidth in the corresponding spectral regions. For UVA, x = 315 nm and y = 400 nm; for UVB, x = 280 nm and y = 315 nm; and for UVC, x = 200 nm and y = 280 nm.

The R-CDs show excellent UV blocking of the light in the UVC (100%) and UVB (99.7%) region and less transmission within the UVA (92.6%) spectral region, while the G-CDs demonstrate a gradual decrease of UV blocking in the UVA (82.5%), UVB (63.6%), and UVC (22.3%) spectral regions (Table 1 ). It is interesting to note that despite the equal mixing ratio between R-CDs and G-CDs in RG-CDs, they exhibit high efficiency blocking values of 99.9%, 99.5%, and 88.4% for UVC, UVB, and UVA blocking, respectively.

Moreover, the transmission spectra were utilized to estimate the energy band gaps of the CDs using the Tauc plot equation, as follows (Aji et al., 2017):
(2)
α h υ = B
(
h υ −
E g
)
1 / 2
where
α
is an absorption coefficient,
h
is Planck's constant,
υ
is the frequency of the incident photon,
E g
is the energy gap of CDs, and
B
is a constant.

The energy gaps of CDs, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6(a), were estimated to be 3.04 eV, 3.43 eV, and 3.97 eV for R-CDs, RG-CDs, and G-CDs, respectively. Reduction in the energy gaps is associated with the quantum confinement effect when the size of CDs increases, and the increase of oxygen and hydrogen contents (Ding et al., 2016). Thus, R-CDs exhibit lower energy gaps caused by the quantum confinement effect and the migration of energy states of CO and C–H/C–OH bonding, inducing a lower energy level than G-CDs. Moreover, the dominant emission centers of R-CDs and G-CDs are in different regions, indicating that the electronic properties of the CDs originate from both the carbon core and the surface functional energy states (Ding et al., 2017).

To gain further information about the photobleaching effect of the CDs, fluorescent emission recombination lifetime analyses were performed. The average lifetimes of R-CDs, RG-CDs, and G-CDs were 6.00 ns, 3.59 ns, and 2.37 ns, respectively (Fig. 6(b)). The longer lifetimes of R-CDs and RG-CDs suggest that the luminescence phenomena of CDs can be attributed to different minority carriers sensitive to oxygen and hydrogen contents, adopting various states (Fig. 5 (d)–(f)).

To examine the performance of CDs as UV-shielding material, the UV response was studied by measuring variations in the absorption and transmission spectra under a 405 nm exposure wavelength for 0–320 min (Fig. 7 ). The results revealed that the average reduction rates in UV light absorption of the main peak were 0.006, 2 × 10−5, and 0.003 a.u./min for R-CDs, G-CDs, and RG-CDs, respectively (insets of Fig. 7(a)–(c)). It is noted that R-CDs exhibited the highest reduction rates. Further study on the percentages of UV shielding revealed radiation-blocking averages of 97.4, 56.1, and 95.9% with average reduction rates of 0.072, 0.001, and 0.003 %/min for R-CDs, G-CDs, and RG-CDs, respectively (insets of Fig. 7(d)–(f)). The G-CDs/PVA has lower absorption and blocking reduction rates compared to the others. This can be described with the FL emission demonstrated in the G-CD solution since the luminescence mainly arose from the CC/C–C energy states. Hence, UV radiation has fewer effects on the stable emission of G-CDs compared to R-CDs. Consequently, combining G-CDs with R-CDs results in enhanced fluorescence characteristics for the resultant RG-CDs. The observed trend is evidenced by the increase in the number of optimized fluorescence peaks, which exhibit a direct correlation with the rise in the energy states (Fig. 5). Furthermore, emissions from the core structure are increased, thereby augmenting the stability and intensity of light absorption within the core structure, leading to a rise in UV blocking. The results demonstrated that RG-CDs exhibit a high percentage of UV blocking and relatively moderate reduction rates, indicating a highly stable blocking performance. Therefore, a combination material such as RG-CDs is most suitable for UV-shielding materials.

In further studies on the pattern of UV response performance in polymer films, nanocomposite films of CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) polymer (CDs/PVA) were prepared by a drop-casting method. The investigation focused on the UV response ability of CD/PVA polymer by considering the mechanism of change in functional groups on the CD surface. The FTIR results observed that the CC bonding (peak at 1639 cm−1) disappeared, while the functional groups C–H, CO, and C–O increased, corresponding to functional surfaces of pure PVA polymer (Fig. 8 (a) and (b)). In addition, the functional groups on the surface of G-CDs/PVA have changed with the appearance of C–OH, which is accompanied by a decreasing energy band gap (from 3.95 eV to 3.92 eV). This demonstrates the remarkable correlations between the R-CDs, G-CDs, and PVA composites, confirming the compatibility and well-dispersed nature of CDs in the PVA films. Moreover, during UV irradiation of R-CD and G-CD solutions, the CO functional groups were increased. However, there was no change in the functional surface groups of both R-CD/PVA and G-CD/PVA films. The fluorescent intensities of R-CDs/PVA, and G-CDs/PVA also showed quenching consistent with FL results for R-CD and G-CD solutions.

The transmission results could be used to estimate the blocking values and energy gaps. The percentage of UV shielding results revealed radiation-blocking averages of 35.0%, 100.0%, and 83.8% for PVA, R-CDs/PVA, G-CDs/PVA before UV exposure, respectively (Fig. 8(c) and Table 1). After UV exposure for 1010 min, average UV blocking values of 100.00%, and 93.47% were demonstrated for R-CDs/PVA and G-CDs/PVA, respectively, consistent with the CDs in solution. The energy gap of PVA was 5.31 eV, while that of R-CDs/PVA increased from 2.95 eV to 3.00 eV, and G-CDs/PVA decreased from 3.95 eV to 3.92 eV after UV exposure (Fig. 8(d)). As a result of the UV exposure, the energy gaps of R-CDs/PVA and G-CDs/PVA tended to converge towards the blue regions. However, the R-CDs/PVA polymer film exhibited a high UV-shielding potential, the same as the R-CDs solution.

In the polymer form, G-CDs exhibited a significant improvement in average UV-blocking effectiveness, rising by 27.7%; conversely, the enhancement observed for R-CDs was only 2.6%. Moreover, the average UV-blocking efficiency of PVA increased by 65.0% with the addition of R-CDs and 48.9% with G-CDs. Therefore, it is notable that PVA supplemented with R-CDs can achieve complete UV blocking, reaching 100.0%.

To utilize CDs as a UV detector, the fluorescence emission response under UV irradiation with an excitation wavelength of 405 nm was studied in both the solutions and nanocomposites of CDs and PVA polymer. In CD solution cases, it was found that the change rates in UV light emission of the main peaks were 0.15, 2.00, and 0.08 a.u./min for R-CDs, G-CDs, and RG-CDs, respectively (Fig. 9 ). With fluorescence results of R-CDs, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity in the red wavelength range of 682 nm–715 nm decreased, while the fluorescence intensity in the green region of 460 nm–540 nm increased (lower inset of Fig. 9(a)). This could result in an emission color change from red to green. However, the maximum fluorescence of G-CD's central wavelength is blue shifted from 487 nm to 451 nm, inducing a color change from green to blue (Fig. 9(b)), while the mixture of RG-CDs exhibited a fluorescence decrease rate between that of G-CDs and R-CDs (Fig. 9 (c)) and found two new emission peaks at 823 nm and 883 nm, resulting in its color change. Therefore, G-CDs have the highest emission response rate to UV radiation.

Under UVA (wavelength of 405 nm) exposure in the R-CD/PVA and G-CD/PVA films, the emission images were changed from red to green and green to blue, respectively (Fig. 10 (a)). UV light detection can be observed from their color changes due to surface-passivated UV radiation. In addition, the image processing in red, green, and blue (RGB) color space of R-CDs/PVA demonstrates increases of green color intensity with decreases of red and blue color intensity. Meanwhile, the color intensity of G-CDs/PVA changed from a blue-green color to a dark blue color observed in the CIE coordinate in Fig. 10(d). These results are consistent; the fluorescence emission spectra of a green emission peak appear at 550 nm for R-CDs/PVA (Fig. 10(e)) and blue shift in G-CDs/PVA (Fig. 10(f)). Thus, the synthesized R-CD/PVA and G-CD/PVA films provide the potential for UV detection in the red to blue range emission.

This research has successfully synthesized FL tunable red and green CDs from the natural material of mangosteen leaves. It utilizes a simple one-step hydrothermal method and fabricates CD/PVA films by the drop-casting method. The results demonstrate that the synthesized CD solutions and CD/PVA films have the potential for UV shielding and sensing. In addition, both R-CD solutions and R-CDs/PVA have outstanding UV-shielding and detection performances. However, the emissions of both R-CDs and G-CDs under UV radiation cover the regions of red, green, and blue emission. Thus, this work illustrates the promise of the development of R-CD- and G-CD-based platforms that can be used in UV-shielding and sensing applications.

Tanachporn Lukprang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Pakorn Preechaburana: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Supaluck Amloy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from Thailand Science Research and Innovation Fundamental Fund fiscal year 2024, Thammasat University (Contract No. TUFF26/2567) and Thammasat University Research Unit in Innovation of Optical Devices and Nanomaterials for Chemical and Biological Sensing. T. Lukprang would like to thank the Doctoral Scholarship for Research Academic, and Internationalization and Education Research Promotion Scholarship from Thammasat University. The authors would like to thank Department of Physics and Department of Materials Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University for providing access to the optical characterization instruments.
